did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) algorithm for classification and ABCD feature extraction (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter) to detect superficial basal cell carcinoma skin cancer.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of an Android-based application for early detection of superficial basal cell carcinoma using ABCD feature extraction and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classification. It details the research methodology, data retrieval (20 images), system design, implementation, and testing results (91.6% accuracy), which constitutes a technical study producing new results rather than a review of prior literature.

title: Early Detection of Superficial Basal-Cell Carcinoma Skin Cancer with Extraction Method ABCD Feature Based on Android
journal: 2019 International Conference on Advanced Mechatronics, Intelligent Manufacture and Industrial Automation (ICAMIMIA)
brief_summary: This study developed an Android-based application for the early detection of superficial basal cell carcinoma skin cancer using ABCD feature extraction and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classification. The dataset consisted of 20 validated images of basal cell carcinoma retrieved from various sources and expert validation. The system extracted features including Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, and Diameter to classify lesions. Performance testing evaluated the impact of light intensity, capture distance, and angle, with angle showing the highest individual accuracy at 90%. The overall feature extraction accuracy achieved 91.6% using KNN with K values of 5, 7, and 9. The results indicate that all four ABCD parameters significantly affect the potential detection of skin cancer.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the study is Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC/KSB), specifically focusing on the superficial subtype for the Android-based detection application. The dataset consisted of 20 images of Basal Cell Carcinoma. Other subtypes (nodular, morpheaformic, pigmented, fibroepitelioma Pinkus) are mentioned in the clinical overview but not explicitly stated as part of the experimental dataset used for the KNN classification.
The dataset included 20 images of facial acne, which served as the 'Not Detected' (negative) class in the classification task alongside the 20 Basal Cell Carcinoma images.

all_task_types.target: Detection of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) vs. Not Detected (e.g., facial acne)
all_task_types.details: The paper uses the K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) algorithm to classify images into discrete diagnostic categories: 'Detected' (Basal Cell Carcinoma) or 'Not Detected' (facial acne/negative class). The task is binary classification based on ABCD feature extraction (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) classification system and feature extraction.
To calculate the distance between test data and training data samples for KNN classification.
To calculate the Border and Color feature parameters during ABCD feature extraction.
To calculate the Asymmetry feature parameter during ABCD feature extraction.

statistical_methods_used.method: K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) with K values of 5, 7, and 9
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To classify images as detected or not detected based on the majority vote of the K nearest neighbors.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported classification accuracy (91.6%) and Euclidean distances for K-Nearest Neighbor classification but did not perform statistical hypothesis testing.

KNN_Classification_Testing.dataset_description: 40 datasets split into 30% (20 Basal Cell Carcinoma images, 20 facial acne images)
KNN_Classification_Testing.notes: The highest accuracy of 91.6% was achieved with K values of 5, 7, and 9. The calculation involved 11 correct training results and 1 incorrect data point out of the split dataset.

Light_Intensity_Testing.dataset_description: Indoor testing using a luxmeter with varying light intensity ranges
Light_Intensity_Testing.notes: The best test results for light intensity were obtained at 100-200 lux.

Image_Capture_Distance_Testing.dataset_description: Indoor testing using a ruler with varying capture distances
Image_Capture_Distance_Testing.notes: The best results for image capture distance were obtained at 10cm.

Image_Capture_Angle_Testing.dataset_description: Indoor testing using an arc with varying capture angles
Image_Capture_Angle_Testing.notes: The best image capture angle was found to be 0 degrees.

ABCD_Feature_Extraction_Testing.dataset_description: Validation of ABCD feature extraction parameters (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter)
ABCD_Feature_Extraction_Testing.notes: The ABCD feature extraction test obtained the greatest accuracy of 91.6% with KNN K values of 5, 7, and 9. All four parameters (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter) affect the potential for skin cancer detection.

architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: ABCD Feature Extraction (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter)

feature_calculation_details.border: Sum of Absolute Differences (SAD) with Gaussian blur filter
feature_calculation_details.color: Sum of Absolute Differences (SAD) with Gaussian blur filter
feature_calculation_details.diameter: Longest line in image converted to mm using DPI constant (2.54/96)

training_details.dataset_composition: 20 Basal Cell Carcinoma images, 20 facial acne images

classification_or_output_head.decision_rule: Majority vote among K nearest neighbors

preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of input images to grayscale
Application of Gaussian blur filter for Border and Color feature extraction
Determination of center of mass (G) for Asymmetry calculation
conclusions: The developed Android-based application is capable of detecting superficial basal cell carcinoma using the ABCD feature extraction method.
The ABCD feature extraction test achieved the highest classification accuracy of 91.6% when using K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) with K values of 5, 7, and 9.
System performance testing identified optimal conditions for image capture: a light intensity of 100-200 lux, a capture distance of 10 cm, and a capture angle of 0 degrees.
Under optimal testing conditions, the system achieved individual accuracies of 75% for light intensity, 80% for image capture distance, and 90% for image capture angle.
Increasing the amount of training data leads to improved classification accuracy.
important_remarks: Extremely small dataset: The study used only 40 images total (20 Basal Cell Carcinoma and 20 facial acne images), which is insufficient for robust machine learning validation.
Lack of external validation: Data was retrieved from various online sources (junks, e-books, websites) and validated by a single expert, with no mention of external or multi-center validation.
No statistical significance testing: The paper reports accuracy percentages but does not provide p-values, confidence intervals, or perform formal hypothesis testing to validate the results.
Limited class diversity: The negative class consisted solely of facial acne, limiting the generalizability of the 'Not Detected' class to other skin conditions or normal skin.
Conventional algorithm: The study relies on traditional K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) with hand-crafted ABCD features rather than deep learning, which may limit performance compared to modern standards.
Sensitivity to capture conditions: Performance varied significantly based on light intensity, distance, and angle, indicating high sensitivity to environmental factors in real-world deployment.

Conflicting demographic data: The introduction cites varying male-to-female ratios (2:1 in one section, 3:2 in another) and age ranges without clarifying the specific population characteristics of the dataset.
No comparison to baseline or state-of-the-art: The paper does not compare its 91.6% accuracy against other established methods or classifiers to contextualize the strength of the evidence.
